profile.summary: A UX Lead sets the direction for user experience across a product area or team. They guide research, interaction design, and design quality, while aligning designers, product managers, engineers, and stakeholders. They often balance hands-on design work with coaching, prioritisation, and influencing decisions without always having formal authority over every contributor.

known_challenges.text: Balancing hands-on design work with leading other designers and shaping standards.
Getting product and engineering partners to protect time for discovery, testing, and iteration.
Carrying design quality across multiple streams of work without becoming a bottleneck.
Managing stakeholder opinions that override user evidence or design rationale.
Sustaining energy while absorbing feedback, ambiguity, and cross-functional tension.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Design leadership versus individual contribution balance
recommended_question_themes.why: Lead-level UX roles often struggle to divide time between doing the work and enabling others to do it well.

recommended_question_themes.theme: Influence with product, engineering, and stakeholders
recommended_question_themes.why: This role depends heavily on persuasion and alignment, especially when design authority is informal.
Understanding whether user insight is shaping choices helps reveal where design is being supported or sidelined.
A lead is usually expected to raise the bar across work, not just on their own projects.
UX leads often carry emotional and coordination load that can drain focus and resilience over time.

terminology.meaning: Always-on user research feeding design, not a one-off phase.
Systems that make user research repeatable and shared.
Quick clickable models to test ideas before building.
Shared components and rules for consistent design.
Using AI tools to draft flows and explore options fast.
Building access in continuously, not as a final check.
A standing team of design, product, and engineering.
Structured peer review of in-progress design work.
Agreeing what the team takes on each short cycle.
Getting decision-makers to agree direction and trade-offs.
Growing the team through feedback, not doing it for them.
Linking design choices to product and business goals.
Judging work by user results, not volume shipped.
Tools and process that scale design across teams.
Holding consistent standards over parallel projects.

listen_for: They talk about trade-offs between user needs, delivery pressure, and technical limits.
They mention repeated rework caused by late feedback or unclear decision ownership.
They describe coaching designers or reviewing work more than producing final designs themselves.
They use evidence-based language when defending choices, or note when evidence is missing.
They sound like the default resolver for cross-team design issues.
avoid: Treating them as only a senior maker and ignoring the leadership part of the role.
Assuming visual polish is the main measure of success instead of user outcomes and team influence.
Pushing them to approve every design detail, which turns them into a bottleneck.
Reading stakeholder conflict as poor collaboration when it may reflect real product trade-offs.
